data.title: Stryker: Solid Growth And Improving Margins Continue To Drive The Stock Higher
data.description:
Stryker's (NYSE:SYK) business has continued to thrive in recent quarters, despite the challenging environment for capital spending and the potential elimination of COVID-related surgical backlogs. This hasn't really benefitted shareholders over the last 12 months, though, as the stock has traded in line with the market.

I previously suggested that margin gains would drive upside for Stryker investors. While the company's operating margin has trended higher in recent quarters, cash flows are trailing somewhat. I expect Stryker's execution and product portfolio to continue driving solid growth, and the company's margins should trend higher in the coming years. I also believe there is modest room for multiple expansion as the strength of Stryker's business becomes more recognized by investors. Stryker's revenue multiple is already reasonably high, though, meaning investors shouldn't expect this to be a primary driver of returns going forward.

Market Conditions

While there are a number of structural tailwinds (aging population, adoption of robotic-assisted surgery, ASC growth) supporting Stryker's business at the moment, there are also challenges that are more transient in nature. This includes the exhaustion of COVID-era patient backlogs, tariffs, and high interest rates.

Procedure volumes were solid in the first quarter and the pricing environment remains stable. Stryker is seeing some pricing pressure on the orthopedic side of its business, though. Rather than 2-3% price erosion annually, Stryker is now seeing 0-2% deflation, which is supported by the shift to ASCs. Stryker has been able to increase pricing on the MedSurg side of its business, though, supported by the value it provides to customers.

While tariffs have increased uncertainty, Stryker has suggested it has up to six months of visibility of capital sales and that it hasn't seen any canceled orders. Stryker expects something like a $200 million headwind from tariffs in 2025, although this estimate is highly dependent on what tariffs are actually implemented and when.

In comparison, Zimmer Biomet's (ZBH) business is struggling in the face of high interest rates, trade tensions, and competition. Much of Zimmer Biomet's recent struggles seem to stem from the strength of Stryker's business, though. Similar to Stryker, Zimmer Biomet's extremities and trauma business is also driving growth at the moment.

While Intuitive Surgical (ISRG) competes in different end markets, its business is still indicative of the overall demand environment and the potential impact of tariffs. Intuitive Surgical has seen strong procedure growth and solid system placements in recent quarters and expects 15-17% procedure growth in 2025. This is despite the fact that bariatric surgeries in the US are still down by a mid-single-digit percentage due to the impact of GLP-1s. This is something worth monitoring, as weight loss could lead to increased activity levels and in time, increased demand for orthopedic surgery. Weight loss can also increase the population eligible for surgery.

Tariffs are expected to increase Intuitive Surgical's cost of sales by approximately 1.7% of revenue in 2025. This cost is rolling through Intuitive Surgical's inventory, though, meaning it will be higher exiting the year. Intuitive Surgical appears set to absorb these cost increases initially, which is not surprising given its cost structure. In the longer term, the company plans on adjusting its supply chain and reassessing pricing.

Stryker Business Updates

Demand is strong within Stryker's ortho business, with surgeons continuing to have elevated backlogs. Stryker expects this market to grow at a 4-5% rate going forward and believes it will continue to take share.

Trauma and Extremities is now the largest business within Stryker's Orthopedics and Spine segment. Trauma and extremities growth in Q1 was reportedly driven by both core trauma and upper extremities. The core trauma business is being driven by the Pangea plating system, which was introduced around 12 months ago. This growth comes despite the fact that Stryker is still in the process of fully launching the Pangea system. It is expected to be released in Australia and Canada in 2025 and Japan in the first half of 2026. Stryker has historically been the leading nailing company but has trailed in plating.

Stryker recently divested its spinal implants business, although did not disclose the sale price. There will reportedly be milestone payments associated with this, though. Zimmer Biomet also divested its spine business, citing weak growth and poor profitability.

Capital sales have been robust, despite high interest rates. Around 10% of Stryker's revenue comes from larger capital items and another 15% from smaller capital items. The sale of smaller capital items is closely tied to procedure volumes, which helps to provide a consistent source of demand.

Mako also remains an area of strength, with Stryker placing a record number of Mako systems during the first quarter. This should lead to market share gains in 6–9 months as these systems become fully utilized.

Stryker recently launched the Mako 4 smart robotic system. It features a larger monitor, smaller footprint, and improved integration with the Q guidance system. Stryker has also added new capabilities to the system, enabling new procedures to be performed with the system.

The full commercial launch of Mako Spine in the US is expected in the second half of 2025. The Mako Shoulder launch is expected in the first quarter of 2026. Early Mako Spine and Shoulder feedback has been positive.

Stryker completed the acquisition of Inari Medical in early 2025, which increased the company's exposure to the fast-growing peripheral vascular segment. Inari provides solutions for venous thromboembolism clot removal and is complementary to Stryker’s Neurovascular business. Inari is reportedly performing well, with its results included within Stryker's vascular division.

Financial Analysis

Stryker generated $5.9 billion of revenue in the first quarter, an increase of roughly 12% YoY. MedSurg and Neurotechnology organic sales growth was 10.7%, while Orthopedics organic sales growth was 9.3%. This growth comes despite a relatively difficult comparable period and one less selling day in the quarter. Pricing provided a 0.7% tailwind in the quarter, while capital sales remained robust. This is supported by an elevated order book across the capital business, driven by Mako. Stryker's hips and knees businesses are outperforming the market at the moment. Trauma and extremities are another area of strength, supported by the introduction of the Pangea plating solution.

Stryker now expects 8.5-9.5% full-year organic sales growth. Guidance is supported by favorable pricing trends, while foreign exchange is expected to be a slight headwind. Stryker's results will no longer include its spinal implants business, beginning in Q2.

Stryker's gross margin was approximately 64% in Q1, while its operating margin was 14%. The burden of operating expenses continues to decline, but this has largely been offset by lower gross margins. This situation is likely to be exacerbated if tariffs come into effect and remain in place. Recent improvements in OpEx have been supported by a reduction in R&D. I expect Stryker's margins to trend higher as it scales and integrates recent acquisitions, which will be an important component of investor returns.

Conclusion

Stryker's growth remains solid, supported in large part by Mako. Capital allocation is also a big part of Stryker's strategy, with inorganic growth continuing to be an important contributor.

Analysts are expecting high single-digit/low double-digit revenue growth in the coming years. I tend to think that these projections are overly pessimistic though, given Stryker's ability to execute, its current product portfolio, and the likely contribution from further M&A.

While Stryker's stock has traded in line with the broader market over the past 12 months, I believe the company is still well-positioned to outperform the market longer term.

Stryker's revenue multiple is now relatively high, but this is justified by the company's margins, which I expect to continue trending higher. A combination of low double-digit revenue growth and a stable or even slightly higher revenue multiple should see Stryker modestly outperform the market going forward.

data.title: Akebia Therapeutics: Finally Showing Potential, But Wait For Q2 Earnings Before Buying
data.description:
Investment Overview

I first covered Akebia Therapeutics (NASDAQ:AKBA) in a note for Seeking Alpha entitled "Vadadustat Approval Seems Make Or Break" all the way back in November 2019.

Vadadustat is an oral HIF prolyl hydroxylase, or HIF-PH, inhibitor, indicated for anemia caused by Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD). In August 2020, Akebia's share price collapsed from >$10 per share to <$2.5 per share, after a pivotal study of the drug missed a safety endpoint, non-inferiority for major adverse cardiovascular events (MACE) (all-cause mortality, non-fatal myocardial infarction, non-fatal stroke).

Orally available and effective, Vadadustat had looked a certainty for FDA approval, but its New Drug Application (NDA) was rejected for approval in March 2022. The FDA also denied subsequent appeals of the decision.

In March last year, however, the FDA finally approved Vadadustat, brand name Vafseo, and Akebia stock has begun to rise, reaching a high of nearly $3 per share in March this year, before the company announced a stock offering on March 19th, to raise ~$50m at $2 per share.

Akebia's current traded share price is $3.6, which values the company at a market cap of $950m. Given that analysts have set a peak revenue target for Vafseo of the same figure, is there a case to be made Akebia stock is undervalued, still trading at a 70% discount to its traded price of >$12, despite Vafseo now having been approved?

In this note, I'll take a deeper dive look at Akebia and its business today and attempt to answer that question.

Akebia – Business Overview & Recent Developments

Akebia earned revenues of $160.2m in 2024, thanks to sales of Auryxia (ferric citrate), indicated for dialysis-dependent CKD, and approved in 2017. Sales were down from $190.2m in 2023, and the drug will lose its patent protection in 2025, likely resulting in further sales declines. Akebia's net loss in 2024 was $(69.4m). The cash position was reported as $52m.

Announcing Q1 earnings on 8th May, Akebia revealed that Vafseo had generated $12m of revenues in the quarter. Akebia discussed progress as follows:

  • Through the end of March, more than 640 prescribers wrote a prescription for Vafseo, and each prescriber, on average, had written nearly 12 prescriptions. About 1/3 of all prescriptions written were refills and refill data demonstrate an increase in average dose per patient.
  • Through the end of April, the top five dialysis organizations placed Vafseo orders, though most revenue continues to be driven by mid-sized dialysis organizations.
  • Akebia estimates that it has at least 12 months of Vafseo inventory on hand in the U.S. free of potential incremental tariff payments based on its current operating plan.

In total, Akebia's Q1 revenues came to $57.3m, up from $32.6m in the prior year quarter, and the company earned a net income of $6.1m, against a net loss of $(18m) in the prior year. The cash position was reported as $113.4m.

Vafseo revenues outperformed guidance set by management for $10m–$11m. Akebia's CEO John Butler told analysts on the Q1 earnings call that:

I believe our strong early performance is a reflection of nephrologists’ desire for a new treatment option to treat anemia in CKD patients on dialysis. Though I continue to express caution that we're in the early stages of product introduction, it is one of the strongest launches I've ever seen in the dialysis market.

The CEO also stated that of the largest two dialysis providers, which treat ~200k patients per annum, one had initiated a pilot program to "operationalize its protocol", while the other was "making the product available on an exception basis for patients".

Regarding insurance coverage, Akebia's Chief Commercial Officer told analysts:

Reimbursement trends remain encouraging. As we previously discussed, we have been initially focused on the access for Medicare Fee-for-Service patients within the TDAPA reimbursement system. We also indicated that we expected Medicare Advantage coverage where dialysis organizations had contracted with Medicare Advantage plans for a TDAPA-like or innovation payment. We continue to see reimbursement outside of Medicare Fee-for-Service.

In summary, then, the Vafseo launch appears to be progressing well—Akebia's share price is also progressing well, up >45% on a one-month basis, although we may note that a first Auryxia generic has already been launched and that Akebia has stopped short of providing financial guidance for 2025.

Akebia has long pointed out (most recently in a presentation given at the JP Morgan Healthcare conference) that the current standards of care in Vafseo's market, erythropoiesis-stimulating agents (ESAs), have been unchanged for >30 years, and that HIF-PH inhibitors—developed using Nobel Prize-winning technology—were their natural successor. As an orally available therapy, Vafseo is additionally advantageous for patients.

Apparently, Vafseo costs ~$15.5k wholesale, and Akebia believes ~150k patients receiving a higher ESA dose "may be associated with all-cause mortality and cardiovascular complications", and may benefit from switching to Vafseo.

Akebia's challenge is to establish Vafseo as the standard of care in dialysis and gain approval in non-dialysis; however, the first is problematic due to lower-priced generic ESAs (is the fact ESAs have been standard of care for >30 years sign patients are broadly happy with using them?), and the second will take time and money, with no guarantee of eventual success.

Vafseo Launch and VALOR Phase 3 Top Near-Term Priorities

Akebia's VALOR study in CKD non-dialysis patients is expected to initiate in the second half of this year, so any approval within the next two years is unlikely, I'd speculate.

The non-dialysis market is worth substantially more than the dialysis market, Akebia believes—worth multi-billions, where dialysis is capped at ~$1bn per annum. This is not related to the number of patients—~500k dialysis versus ~550k non-dialysis, Akebia states, but due to the higher net pricing available from "predominantly private and unbundled government payment" in non-dialysis. Akebia's research also suggests that "frustration with ESA use" is "driving declining treatment rates in CKD non-dialysis anemia".

Further ahead, Akebia is targeting the acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS) market with vadadustat, with a Phase 2a study underway. The company believes this is a ~200k patient market with a total addressable market (TAM) of $2bn.

A next-gen HIF-PH inhibitor is being positioned for a shot at approval in cardiac surgery-associated acute kidney injury (CS-AKI), while another, in the rare disease retinopathy of prematurity (ROP), is a ~30k patient market.

Looking Ahead - Can Akebia Finally Succeed With Vadadustat?

Akebia easily beat analysts' expectations in Q1, but I have to admit I am surprised no formal 2025 guidance was provided (or, apparently, requested by analysts during the earnings call).

After net losses of $(144m), $(280m), $(385m), and $(282m) between 2018–2021, Akebia has steadied the ship, reporting net losses of $94m, $52m, and $69m between 2022–2024.

Funding the Phase 3 VALOR study will likely be a drain on resources, however, so I doubt Akebia will remain profitable near-to-medium term, especially with Auryxia revenues now likely to fall significantly in every subsequent quarter.

Theoretically speaking, there seem to be few impediments to the wide-scale adoption of Vafseo, which is approved in multiple countries, with plenty of real-world evidence of its efficacy, and importantly, given the delay in approving the drug in the US, safety profile.

That being the case, it may seem probable that the VALOR study can be successful, and even if it takes 2–3 years to secure approval in that market, it's possible that Vafseo could become a “blockbuster” (>$1bn revenues per annum) drug.

Akebia stock has surged in value by >210% over the past 12 months, achieving its highest value in more than four years, so should investors play a "wait and see" game, perhaps waiting for Q2 earnings to be released (they are due 31st July) to see if the launch momentum has been maintained?

All things considered, I'd consider this to be the optimal strategy. It risks losing out on a post-Q2 earnings spike, but we will have more information on, for example, whether the two largest dialysis providers are planning to use Vafseo more and more, what the repeat prescription data looks like, and also, perhaps, whether a first patient has been dosed in the VALOR study and what feedback on that has been received from the FDA.

The fact that Vafseo has been approved in Europe—where it is marketed and sold by Akebia's partner MEDICE Arzneimittel Pütter GmbH—and in Japan, also marketed and sold by a partner, Mitsubishi Tanabe Pharma Corporation, seems to allay any concerns around the safety of the product, even if Fibrogen's (FGEN) roxadustat, which had the same mechanism of action (MoA), has still not made it market in the US.

As such, Akebia's earnings releases this year ought to be the key determinant of value—drum up triple-digit revenues for Vafseo in 2025, and we may see the share price jump, but if the figure is <$50m, factoring in the possibility that Auryxia revenues could dip <$100m, and losses extend >$(100m), we could be looking at a shrinking valuation.

I'm prepared to see what Q2 results bring before diving into any investment.

data.title: SPY Vs. IJR: This Chart Shows It's Time To Buy Small Caps
data.description:
SPY and IJR ETF: chart of the day

I last wrote on the SPDR® S&P 500 ETF Trust (NYSEARCA:SPY) in late April. That article was titled "SPY Fund Flows Suggest Near-Term Stability For The S&P 500.” As the title already hinted, the focus of that article was to analyze the broader equity market via the latest fund flow data from the SPY ETF. That article ended with a hold rating for SPY.

Since then, there have been a few materials developments that have changed two of the most important macroeconomic parameters in my view: S&P 500 Index (SP500) valuation and also treasury rates. As to be detailed later, the key developments that caused these changes are the earnings reported by the constituent stocks in SPY for FQ1 2025 and also the recent downgrade of the U.S. credit rating by Moody’s. For readers unfamiliar with this development, in mid-May, Moody’s Ratings announced the downgrade of the U.S. credit rating due to concerns of its deficits. The announcement immediately caused reactions in the bond markets and pushed up treasury rates.

The above changes in SPY valuation and treasury rates, when combined, let me to the following chart of the day. My goal with this chart is to benchmark the risk premium of SPY by risk-free rates (taken as the 10-year treasury bond rate here). More specifically, I am plotting the CAPE yield of SPY in excess of 10-year rates (US10Y). As seen, SPY’s current excess CAPE yield is around negative 1.75% (based on a CAPE ratio of 36.72x for SPY and 10-year treasury rates of 4.47% as of this writing). This is the lowest level since the dot.com bubble, indicating an unusual level of risk premium entailed by SPY and motivating the search of alternative assets.

Fortunately, our research shows that the risk premium in the equity market is not uniformly distributed and there are viable alternative ideas. One of our top ideas is the iShares Core S&P Small-Cap ETF (NYSEARCA:IJR). As a background, we last analyzed IJR in late Feb in an article tilted “IJR Vs. IWM: IJR Is A Better Bet For The Return Of Small-Cap Stocks.” That article focuses on the “ETF aspects” of IJR when compared with its close peer IWM and concluded with a buy rating for IJR. In this article, I will switch the prospect to be more macro-oriented and explain why IJR (or other similar small cap funds) offers more attractive risk/return ratio than large caps funds such as SPY. In the end, these considerations have led me to maintain my buy rating for IJR and hold rating for SPY.

SPY and IJR ETF: basic facts

SPY needs little introduction. It is one of the most well-established funds that tracks the S&P 500 Index (see more details of it in the fund description).

SPY Fund description: The fund tracks the massively popular US index, the S&P 500. Few realize that S&P's index committee chooses 500 securities to represent the US large-cap space - not necessarily the 500 largest by market cap, which can lead to some omissions of single names. Still, the index offers outstanding exposure to the US large-cap space.

IJR, on the other hand, needs a few more words. It tracks a market-cap-weighted index of 600 small-cap U.S. stocks. As pointed out in its fund description below, these stocks represent only a small sample of the small-cap space (about 3% of the publicly available market) and could give the impression of under-representation.

IJR fund description: The fund tracks the S&P SmallCap 600 Index. As a part of BlackRocks Core ETFs, the fund is often used as a portfolio building block. IJR holds just a fraction of the names in the small-cap space, giving the fund an appearance of being under-diversified. However, the fund still reflects the market quite accurately in both performance and coverage. IJR's avoidance of the most illiquid small-cap names may help explain its strong liquidity.

However, my view is that with its effective sampling algorithm, ~600 holdings are sufficient to cover the space accurately. Furthermore, a key issue with small-cap stocks is liquidity. But limiting itself to the more liquid stocks in this space, IJR strikes a tradeoff that I personally like between liquidity and diversification. Along this line, potential investors should consider the all-in costs (in addition to the expense ratios on the surface) when comparing IJR to similar funds (such as IWM) by factor in the trading frictions hidden within the funds themselves. Lastly, IJR’s expense fees are also among the lowest to my knowledge both within the small-cap space and also in general. For example, its 0.06% expense ratio is lower than SPY’s 0.09% by 1/3.

SPY and IJR ETF: valuation risks in focus

With the above background, let me get back to valuations risks of the equity market. Since my last writing, almost all of S&P 500 companies (more than 98% of them) have reported the earnings results for Q1 2025. According to the following statistics from FactSet:

For Q1 2025, the blended (year-over-year) earnings growth rate for the S&P 500 is 13.3%. 78% of S&P 500 companies have reported a positive EPS surprise and 64% of S&P 500 companies have reported a positive revenue surprise.

The robust earnings growth was accompanied by substantial SPY price appreciation too. As a net result, the S&P 500 is valued (with a P/E of around 28.48x as of this writing) far above its historical average P/E ratio (with a 5-year average of 19.9x and a 10-year average of 18.4). Broadening the horizon, the valuation premium is more concerning as shown in the first chart when benchmarked against risk-free rates or in the following chart in terms of the CAPE ratio.

As aforementioned, the good news is that the valuation risk is not uniformly distributed and there are still sectors attractive priced within the equity market. Small-cap stocks represented by IJR are one of my most attractive candidate on our radar. As a reflection of their attractiveness, the next chart displays the dividend yield spread between IJR and SPY. As seen, historically, this spread has fluctuated between ~1% and -1% most of the time in the past 2~3 decades. The average yield spread is -0.633 in the period between ~2000 and now. The current yield spread hovers around 1.01%, among the highest levels in this long period, indicating IJR’s unusually attractive valuation relative to SPY.

SPY and IJR ETF: dividends and growth in focus

With the above broader picture, the next chart shows more details of the dividends for IJR and the SPY, which I rely on as an indicator of their true economic earnings. As seen, IJR currently provides a TTM Dividend Yield of 2.21%, which is substantially above its 4-year average yield of 1.53% by more than 44%, signaling a valuation discount compared to its own historical levels too. As for SPY, its current yield of 1.20% is noticeably below its historical average yield of 1.37% by about 13%, providing another signal of overvaluation.

Finally, note that IJR also featured a faster dividend growth in the past with a 3-Year CAGR of 12.52% and a 5-Year CAGR of 14.51%. Both rates considerably surpassed SPY's respective growth rates of 7.26% and 4.35%.

Other risks and final thoughts

As passively index funds, both IJR and SPY are subject to the same set of macroeconomics risks such as the ongoing trade disputes, interest rate uncertainties, recession risks, etc. So, here I will focus more on the risks that are unique to them. The top one that stands out is IJR’s volatility risks. Most investors probably already know that small-cap stocks fluctuate more than large-cap stocks. This is certainly true for the case of IJR and SPY. More importantly, the difference is more pronounced during stressful market downturns as you can clearly see from the following chart.

As a recent example, during the COVID-19 start period (Jan 2020 - Mar 2020), SPY experienced a drawdown of -19.43%, while IJR faced a much more significant decline of -32.77%. Looking at other notable drawdowns, we see the same trend. For SPY, its next largest recent drawdown occurred from January 2022 to September 2022, lasting 9 months with a -23.93% drop. For IJR, the next most severe drawdown shown was from September 2018 to March 2020, which both lasted longer (1 year and 7 months) and also suffered a severe -36.12% decline.

To close, this analysis is promoted by two developments since my last analysis: the earnings reported SP500 companies for FQ1 2025 and the recent downgrade of the U.S. credit rating. These developments have both SP500 valuation and treasury rates materially and pushed SPY’s valuation premium to an unusual level according to our model. The good news is that we see viable alternative ideas and IJR is a top idea from our research. To drive the point home, let me also wrap up with a macroscopic picture as shown below. This dashboard is a tool our Investing Group Service (Envision Early Retirement) relies on read the market’s pulses. It’s as a Google sheet and is downloadable. As seen, small-cap stocks represented by IJR is currently the most attractively priced sector ranked by dividend yield Z-score. It’s yield Z-score is positive 3.07, in stark contrast to the very negative Z-score of -2.40 when all market sectors are averaged.

data.title: THYF: Preserves Capital While Collecting Superior Income
data.description:
Overview

T. Rowe Price U.S. High Yield ETF (NYSEARCA:THYF) is an income fund that primarily invests in a diversified portfolio of corporate debt securities. If you are an investor who values income stability, I believe that some exposure to credit investments is essential. Credit investments can be a great way to offset the volatility that traditional equities experience because of the straightforward approach to providing value. Corporate debt securities don't require the underlying borrower to consistently grow earnings or be innovative in any way. As long as the underlying borrowers can continue to pay down their debt and support interest payments, credit investments can be beneficial for investors.

THYF is still relatively new, with an inception dating back to October 2022. The fund has an expense ratio of 0.56% and is managed by T. Rowe Price Group. Looking at the performance since inception, we can see that THYF's price has remained fairly stable. When including all distributions that have been paid out since inception, the total return jumps up to about 25.6%. The main appeal of a fund like THYF is the generous starting dividend yield of about 7.2%. The fund has already proven that it can efficiently preserve investor capital while providing consistent income.

Even though the ETF's performance has been relatively strong since its inception, investors must keep in mind that there are some tradeoffs you'll make when including THYF as part of your portfolio. One of the clearest tradeoffs is that there should be no expectations for THYF to provide capital appreciation. Additionally, the structure of the ETF's focus on debt securities means that it can be vulnerable to shifts in the interest rate environment. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategy and investment methodology implemented by THYF.

Fund Strategy

According to THYF's fact sheet, the fund has total assets of $594M that is spread across 93 different issuers. THYF invests at least 80% of its assets in high yield bonds and uses a fundamental bottom-up process to select its credit investments. The fund managers aim to achieve a high total return while providing consistent income generation. I like the fact that THYF remains diversified across a wide range of different industries, which helps reduce any sort of concentration risk to any one specific focus area.

We can see that exposure to Investment & Financial Services accounts for the largest bulk of assets at 4.83%. This is followed by exposure to Gas Distribution and Energy investments, making up 3.61% and 3.5% of assets respectively. I like the fact that the top three industries make up less than 12%, which reinforces the fact that THYF is very diverse. Additionally, the positions have an average effective duration of 2.82 years, which means that THYF should remain stable in the near term.

It should be understood that THYF should only be implemented by investors that are seeking a source of dividend income. The focus on income generation means that investors shouldn't have any expectations for the fund to provide capital appreciation. Just to demonstrate, we can see how THYF's total return since inception, significantly underperforms the total return delivered by a more traditional ETF like SPDR S&P 500 (SPY). Therefore, THYF should only be used as an addition to an already diverse portfolio of assets. THYF may also be a good fit for retired investors that want to start living off the income generated within their portfolio.

Risk Profile & Outlook

The main source of risk from a fund like THYF would be from the probability of defaults from the underlying loans. This is due to the fact that THYF focuses on borrowers with below investment grade credit ratings, otherwise known as 'junk bonds'. For reference, loans with a credit rating below a BBB are considered to be below investment grade. The breakdown of THYF is as follows:

  • Investment Grade (AAA - BBB): 1.8%
  • Below Investment Grade (BB - D): 84.5%
  • Reserves: 4.3%
  • Bank Debt: 9.4%

Borrowers with below investment grade credit ratings typically have less consistent cash flows, more debt on the balance sheet, or may be less resilient to shifts in consumer spending. Additionally, the higher interest rate environment can be beneficial in terms of income generation for THYF; A higher interest rate means that a fund like THYF can collect a higher amount of interest income from borrowers. For reference, interest rates still remain near their decade highs.

Higher interest rates could also be a source of risk for credit investments because the higher cost of debt puts pressure on underperforming borrowers. A higher cost of debt may negatively impact operating margins and lead to a higher rate of defaults. As you'd expect, defaults are more common for debt that falls within the below investment grade range.

The Fed has decided to keep interest rates unchanged during their latest meeting, as they continue to measure economic data and keep an eye on inflationary data. In my opinion, it doesn't seem like markets are aligned to see interest rates fall much lower, so this risk of defaults could be relevant going into 2026.

Markets are still trying to assess the full impact of President Trump's tariffs. I anticipate that the higher cost of goods will ultimately slow economic growth and lead to higher inflation rates. President Trump recently stated that China has violated trade agreements with the US, so there's the potential for even more uncertainty ahead. Therefore, I anticipate that the Fed will keep interest rates unchanged for 2025. Even if I am wrong and interest rates are slightly reduced, I don't think a slight reduction would be enough to really provide relief to underperforming borrowers.

Just as a point of reference, S&P Global has a base scenario estimate that default rates could increase to 1.6% by December 2025. However, I believe that we are more aligned with the pessimistic scenario that sees defaults increasing to 3% by December 2025. This would be linked to challenging consumer spend across industries, slower economic growth, and the possibility that trade wars escalate throughout the next two quarters.

Distribution & Comparison

As of the most recently declared monthly distribution of approximately $0.33 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 7.2%. Looking at the dividend history so far, we can see that payouts have remained fairly consistent since THYF's inception. While the payouts may vary slightly by a few cents, the range has remained consistent. The steady nature of the payouts makes THYF a great fund for investors that want to maintain a long position and don't want to stress about cuts.

However, THYF isn't the type of ETF that will deliver any sort of meaningful dividend growth. On the bright side, the steady range of the payouts also means that investors would be able to essentially create their own dividend growth. This can be achieved by reinvesting portions of the distribution back into THYF over time, which would allow rapid accumulation of shares. This may be a good strategy for investors that are nearing retirement and want to start focusing on a reliable source of income.

While I couldn't locate any specific distribution documents to confirm, investors should assume that all income received from THYF is classified as ordinary dividends. Income received from credit funds are typically fueled by net investment income or net realized gains, which are classified as ordinary dividends. Ordinary dividends have the heaviest tax burden and therefore, a position in THYF may be best utilized within a tax advantaged account. I was able to locate the following statement in the prospectus.

A distribution may consist of ordinary dividends, capital gains, and return of capital. Sales of fund shares and distributions by the fund generally may be taxed as ordinary income or capital gains unless you invest through a tax-deferred account (in which case you will be taxed upon withdrawal from such account).

However, the sustainability of the distributions may change in the future when interest rates are reduced. A lower interest rate may translate to THYF collecting a lesser amount of income from its underlying investments. As mentioned though, I don't think this is something that investors will need to worry about for the remainder of 2025.

Although the fund has been a consistent performer, THYF seems to underperform alternative choices in the market. At random, I pulled in some other high yield bond ETFs that implement a similar approach to THYF. These include:

  • SPDR Portfolio High Yield Bond ETF (SPHY)
  • SPDR Bloomberg High Yield Bond ETF (JNK)
  • iShares Broad USD High Yield Corporate Bond ETF (USHY)

As we can see below, THYF underperforms all of these peers in both price growth and total return since its inception. However, THYF is the newest fund, so there's a chance that performance could look different over a longer time horizon. SPHY has an inception dating back to 2012, JNK goes back to 2007, and USHY has been around since 2017.

These peers also offer a pretty similar income potential to THYF. We can see that SPHY has a slightly higher dividend yield as well. I am not saying that these alternative ETFs are inherently better, but it's good to get a point of reference to compare THYF against going forward. I plan to revisit THYF in the future to compare performance through a lower interest rate environment.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I rate THYF as a buy due to the likelihood that we will remain in a higher interest rate environment. As the tariff related trade wars continue, there's no telling how markets will react throughout the remainder of 2025. If investors wanted a position to hedge traditional equities, THYF offers a compelling return profile. So far, the fund has been able to preserve investor capital since its inception while providing a high yield. The distributions are paid out on a monthly basis and the amounts have remained in a consistent range. I anticipate that payouts can continue at the same level as long as we remain in a higher interest rate environment. However, we still haven't seen how THYF performs in a lower interest rate environment, so I plan to revisit this ETF in the future.

data.title: RSPD: Reasons To Pursue And Reasons To Avoid
data.description:
ETF Snapshot

As the name suggests, the Invesco S&P 500 Equal Weight Consumer Discretionary ETF (NYSEARCA:RSPD), is a passively managed ETF, which tracks an index that focuses on an equally weighted basket of consumer discretionary (CD) stocks, from the prime equity bellwether. RSPD, which currently covers over 50 CD stocks, has been following its remit for close to two decades now (it was set up in November 2006), during which it has managed to amass AUM of around $200m.

For the uninitiated, the broad CD universe is comprised of businesses involved in automobiles, household durables, leisure equipment, clothing & apparel, luxury goods, hospitality & dining, distributors, broadline & specialty retail and diversified consumer services.

An Ambivalent Stance on RSPD

After having reviewed RSPD, we are not too moved by the capabilities of this product. However, we don’t believe it is a washout either, as it still offers some useful features that may appeal to a certain type of investors.

In order to drive home our points more effectively, we have measured RSPD against The Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLY). XLY, we feel, is a good benchmark because it also passively tracks an index which is essentially the modified market-cap weighted version of RSPD’s tracking index. Put another way, XLY too focuses on CD stocks from the S&P 500, but weighs them on the basis of their market-cap. Another reason to stack RSPD up against XLY is because the latter is also easily the most popular and established CD ETF in the market with AUM of over $21 billion (over 100x larger than RSPD, and 4x larger than the next biggest CD ETF-VCR), and a listing history of 27 years!

Nonetheless, we’ve chosen to employ a neutral stance on RSPD, but here’s a summary of what’s good and bad about this product, and the CD sector, for those who may be contemplating a position in the same.

What’s Good?

One of the major incentives to pursue something like an RSPD is that it is a more well-balanced play on what can be perceived to be a rather overheated and steeply priced sector.

To highlight, as things stand, the CD sector is currently the most expensive sector around, priced at a forward P/E of 28x, a 32% premium over the S&P 500.

Note also that the relative strength (RS) ratio of market-cap weighted consumer discretionary stocks (versus the S&P 500), is currently perched at a 15% premium over the long-term relative strength average, leaving it susceptible to some downward mean-reversion.

Also note that even though XLY covers around 50 stocks, its ten largest stocks account for a whopping 70% of the total portfolio. In RSPD’s case, this top-heaviness is not present, with the top 10 only accounting for 24% in aggregate.

RSPD is also more well-spread out, and tilts more towards the mid-cap segment of the CD universe (which, in theory, are supposed to offer a superior long-term earnings growth runway than giant and large-caps). To elaborate, over 53% of RSPD’s stocks come from the mid-cap segment, in addition to some handy exposure to small-caps. XLY, on the other hand, is heavily skewed towards giant-and-large-caps that account for well over 75% of the portfolio. Small-cap exposure is minimal at just 2%.

Nonetheless, note that RSPD’s mid-cap heavy portfolio is currently priced at a rather compelling P/E multiple of less than 18x; conversely, XLY is priced at a +50% premium at over 27x. At this superior multiple, one would expect XLY’s holdings to offer higher long-term earnings growth, but ironically its RSPD’s mid-cap and small-cap tilted portfolio that come out on top (earnings growth potential of almost 160bps more).

Since these are passively managed products that track certain indices, you also want these ETFs to closely mirror the performance of their respective indices. To get a better sense of this, one may take note of the tracking error of these two products. Rather than utilizing a ‘representative sampling’ process, RSPD follows a ‘full replication methodology’ in tracking its target index, which essentially ensures a much lower tracking error across time.

Most investors who typically pursue CD sector products don’t normally focus on the income profile of this sector, as it is noted for reinvesting excess cash back into growing the business (rather than doling it out by way of distribution). But yet still, for those who do, RSPD has been offering a marginally better yield than XLY since 2022, with the current differential working out to 7bps (at 0.95).

What’s Bad?

Cost accessibility is undoubtedly a pivotal variable for investors looking to play the ETF game; while XLY can be accessed at a compellingly low expense ratio of only 0.08%, the same can’t’ be said of RSPD that is priced at an expense ratio of exactly 5x more (0.4%)

It has been well-documented that past returns in the markets are not an indication of future results, but yet still, RSPD’s “magnitude” of underperformance relative to XLY, over time, should still cause a fair degree of trepidation amongst prospective investors.

Note that since RSPD got listed in 2006, it has only managed to generate roughly half the returns that XLY has facilitated.

That isn’t all; one should also take note of both the long and short-term Sharpe ratios, as it shows how well these two products have managed the risk they’ve taken, in their efforts to outperform risk-free products. Once again, it is clear that RSPD has the inferior risk-adjusted return track record.

Then, leaving aside the structural merits and demerits of these two ETF products, it is also questionable if current conditions are conducive enough for consumer discretionary stocks to flourish.

The United States Michigan Consumer Expectations survey has been in contractionary territory (readings below 50) for the last couple of months, and suggests that spending momentum going forward will be rather low (for context, the average reading over the last 73 years has been 79 points).

This can be validated even further from the latest Deloitte Consumer Insight survey which came out at the end of last month, showing that spending intentions for discretionary categories continues to drift lower and is below 2021 levels.

One wouldn’t expect U.S. consumers to engage in a great deal of spending, when their financial well-being is not in the best of health; this is reiterated by the Deloitte financial well-being index, which continues to dip every other month.

Besides a tricky financial landscape, it’s also fair to say that the recent tariff shenanigans have made U.S. consumers even more wary; over 60% of U.S. consumers are planning to change or have changed their spending habits, with over half of them curtailing spending in non-essential categories which would cover a lot of what these CD businesses provide.

The c-suite of these CD businesses are not wearing blinders, and they too appear to be forecasting things with far less ebullience. In fact, note that a bulk of the CD stocks of the S&P500 (around 85%) have actually provided negative guidance for the upcoming Q2 earnings (only the material sector has a higher proportion of negative guidance).

It isn’t just Q2, even looking ahead to the FY, earnings growth this year looks very underwhelming at less than 1% (two months back, the expectations were much higher at around mid-single-digits). Crucially, that cadence of earnings is one of the weakest amongst all sectors (only staples and energy are expected to provide a lower degree of earnings growth).

Summary

To conclude, while we don’t believe the equally weighted, and cheaply valued RSPD is the most egregious CD ETF around, its underwhelming risk-adjusted track record, relatively high expense ratio, and an unsavory backdrop for the CD sector as a whole, make it a risky bet at this juncture.

data.title: Ternium: Strong Buy Based On Deep Value And Market Pessimism
data.description:
Strong Buy on Deep Value; Structural Pessimism Overdone

We initiate on Ternium S.A. (NYSE: NYSE:TX) with a Strong Buy rating and $45 PT. Ternium S.A., is Latin America’s leading steel and mining platform, manufacturing and distributing steel products across twelve countries across construction, automotive, energy and other industrial end-markets. Our investment thesis is underpinned by three main arguments:

(1) we think the market is mispricing a deeper longer revenue trough with our 2025E revenue forecast of $16.2bn 4% below consensus on both Mexican and Brazilian demand weakness and regional steel spreads compression with management guiding volume flat to down and only double-digit EBITDA margins not growth,

(2) although the volume headwinds in the short term and the asset ramp-up in the longer term limits a more secular growth story in our view the 0.27x EV/Sales is embedding extreme pessimism and even assuming a rather conservative mean reversion to our 0.45x 2026E forward multiple is justified given Ternium’s product mix, geographic diversification and supply chain execution,

(3) our $45 PT implies 68% upside and is justified by multiple expansion and cash generation as incremental capacity and US infra spending gradually support recovery. We flag that a further macro deterioration or a prolonged Chinese export pressure could depress regional steel pricing and delay earnings recovery that we factor in our below consensus revenue and conservative multiple assumptions. Verdict: near-term pessimism is providing a good entry point and disciplined scenarios on the risk as an asymmetric R/R for long-term value investors.

The transformational bet: Ternium’s $4bn+ Mexico investment generates asymmetric structural upside

For investors, Ternium’s unprecedented $4.1bn+ capital commitment to its Pesquería industrial center represents a unique and structural story. This is where it all happens: Ternium’s largest CapEx program in its corporate history sees $2.6bn of next-generation upstream capacity in EAF and DRI, and finishing, pickling, cold rolling, and galvanizing lines, set to be commissioned between late 2025 and Q4 2026. The implications are significant:

1) It catapults Ternium into the value-added segment, with new lines focused on customized products, a 550kt push-pull pickling line, and a 1.6mt cold rolling mill, targeting high-end auto and industrial markets, while a state-of-the-art DRI/EAF setup enables the supply of exposed-grade automotive steel at a fraction of legacy emissions.

2) Self-sufficiency drives margin expansion: the executive team is clear that operational efficiency gains, cost savings, and reduced dependence on external slab sourcing will position Ternium for durable margin expansion as regional volumes normalize.

3) USMCA-compliance (melted and poured) is the competitive moat. Ternium is building this moat ahead of schedule, uniquely positioning itself to capture incremental demand as nearshoring accelerates and high-content North American steel becomes mandatory for customers. The size of the opportunity? With $1.4bn already invested and final phases commissioning, Ternium is poised to benefit not only from a steel cycle recovery but from a fundamental re-architecture of its cost base and product mix, unlocking structural upside that competitors in the Americas cannot easily replicate. Investors looking for an underappreciated capital deployment story with transformative potential need to consider Ternium’s Mexican expansion.

Fortress Balance Sheet: Ternium’s Financial Firepower is the Underappreciated Muscle Behind Its Asymmetric Upside

The math is compelling: Ternium distinguishes itself in the steel space not only for operational acumen, but for its fortress balance sheet and resilient cash generation that provide both downside protection and unique offensive capabilities. With net cash position of $1.3 bn as of 31 March 2025 (after accelerating quarterly CapEx to $518 mn to fund the marquee Mexico expansion), liquidity of $1.8 bn in cash and $1.9 bn in other investments (supporting working capital requirements and flexibility), and operating cash flow of $207 mn in Q1 despite working capital outflows, the company’s cash conversion engine is exemplarily disciplined even at the trough of the steel cycle.

Compelling: Ternium generated operating cash flow of $1.9 bn and paid out over $609 mn in sector-leading dividends in 2024, without balance sheet strain, while still progressing on CapEx at nearly $1.9 bn. The mechanism is engineered efficiency, whether in costs, inventory management, or “pay-as-you-go” growth CapEx, ensuring that high dividend distributions and CapEx can coexist without the toxic leverage that throttles peers in downturns.

With net cash coverage of $1.3 bn and adjusted free cash flow turning firmly positive as new capacity ramps up, Ternium is not only positioned to weather further macro shocks, but to flexibly fund opportunistic investments and continue best-in-class shareholder returns, well ahead of regional volume recovery. For investors looking for asymmetric R/R opportunities in basic materials, this financial firepower is more than just a margin of safety — it is the hidden muscle that turns structural pessimism into a generational buying opportunity.

Ternium S.A.: We model trashed revenue trough and recovery vs the street

We model Ternium’s FY25E revenue of US$16.2 bn (4% below consensus), followed by a 7.5% decline vs FY24, and a muted recovery to US$17.1 bn in FY26E, leaving us materially below the street. Our thesis is well below consensus, as we see three underappreciated negatives:

Source: FMP FY25 FY26
Estimate # Analysts Estimate # Analysts
Revenue $16.86B 7 $17.48B 10
EBITDA $3.40B 7 $3.52B 10
Net Income $830.12M 7 $1.10B 10
EPS $4.23 4 $4.91 6
SG&A Expenses $1.05B 7 $1.09B 10

(1) much weaker and longer-than-modeled steel demand and steel pricing in Mexico and Brazil (Q1-25 revenue in Mexico is already 18% below Q1-24, and management continues to guide mid double-digit EBITDA margin at flat volumes, not at growth),

(2) slower-than-expected ramp-up of new cold-rolling and finishing capacity, with new assets only contributing significantly to top-line late into FY26, and ramp-up of incremental output only partially offsetting third-party slab sales once the internal slab mill comes online,

(3) still-record China steel exports keeping regional prices depressed through 2025-26, as opposed to the street’s view of a rapid price recovery. Unless key risks play out (such as US-Mexico tariffs resolution, or global price boom), we believe the street is too optimistic and see little upside to reported revenues.

Valuation

Our $45 price target for Ternium S.A. is based on a 0.45x forward EV / Sales multiple applied to our below-consensus FY26E revenue forecast of $17.1bn, which implies 68% upside and drives our Strong Buy rating. While the multiple is a rational step up from today's depressed 0.27x, it still implies a material 10% discount to TX's historical median (0.5x) and a significant 25% discount to peer median (0.6x), thereby accounting for continued sector headwinds while leaving room for mean reversion.

We base our valuation on a realistic fundamental and technical framework: we believe Ternium is facing a deeper and longer-than-expected trough in revenues, that consensus is underestimating the duration of structural end-market softness in the region and asset ramp, and our chosen valuation multiple reflects the company's resilient product mix, superior integration and long-dated optionality from US infrastructure stimulus and supply rationalization. Importantly, our shallow multiple expansion assumption reflects our ex ante inclusion of ongoing Chinese export pressure and a structurally more challenging pricing environment, both of which we believe consensus is failing to appropriately incorporate.

Risks to our Strong Buy thesis

Downside risks to our Strong Buy thesis on Ternium S.A. focus primarily on macro shocks, structural policy error and/or significant delay to the expected earnings/utilization recovery deferring mean reversion embedded in our valuation. At the core of our contrarian stance is an assumption that Chinese overflow steel exports are not structurally exacerbated, and regional trade protectionism (primarily Mexico and Brazil), is more effective than expected in defending against margin-destructive imports; a failure to deliver by LatAm authorities, or a global rollback of such measures would weigh on regional steel prices for longer and defer our expected multiple expansion thesis.

The second major risk is executional: Ternium confronts material cost inflation and project risk on its $4bn Pesquería and downstream expansion, where even modest incremental capex overruns and construction slippage, or downstream ramp delay (already to late-2026), could impact expected volume and cash flow inflection points, especially if Mexican industrial demand fails to materialize or US nearshoring and infrastructure end-markets disappoint. Even near-term, key negative catalysts include macro deterioration in Mexico or Brazil, prolongation of the USMCA tariff standoff, unanticipated FX volatility impacting USD-reported earnings, or adverse headlines such as major environmental / ESG / local strike disruption at Ternium's Mexican mining assets.

If forward-looking monitorables such as Q3-25 revenue/ton, new line utilization, progress on US/MX trade resolution, or Chinese steel export growth, persistently move against scenario ranges defined in our risk framework, this would erode the asymmetric R/R favoring our Strong Buy.

Conclusion

We reiterate our Strong Buy view on Ternium S.A., as we believe the market's extreme pessimism on Latam steel cyclicality and delayed asset ramp-up more than reflects in valuation, offering an atypically attractive entry point for long-term investors. Our below-consensus revenue and conservative multiple expansion still leaves significant potential upside, in our view, as Ternium's resilient earnings power and integration optionality remain underappreciated in the face of near-term macro noise. For sophisticated investors willing to look beyond volatility and towards silent compounding, we see this as a classic deep value play with asymmetric upside that compels us to act with conviction at current levels.

data.title: Chart Industries: End Of An Era
data.description:
Jill Evanko, CEO of Chart Industries (NYSE:GTLS) is leading the company to a merger with Flowserve (NYSE:FLS). While the last article has dealt largely with the effects of the Howden acquisition. That is now in the past. The big deal here is that someone like Jill Evanko, CEO (President and a Director also) had a record of value creation that is going to be darn hard to keep going. Such people are generally a rarity to begin with. She will become Chairman of the Board in the newly combined company. The combined company President and CEO, Scott Rowe, will have quite a challenge to keep that record of value creation going.

As the transaction shows, this will be the end of Chart as it is currently composed. The merger with Flowserve will create a new entity going forward. At some point this likely should have been expected because the group in charge of Chart was not getting any younger and the company had to consider a succession strategy. This appears to be that succession strategy.

This is probably the first time that Chart Industry investors have heard anything about investment grade. Chart, for a long time was likely too small and too cyclical to talk about investment grade.

It did not help that all the acquisitions and mergers kept materially affecting the company prospects. Now, the company is evidently going to be large enough and diversified enough that investment grade will become a realistic goal of this merger.

This merger continues a long-held goal of management to diversify away from the dependency of large (multiyear) natural gas projects. Long-time investors may well remember that back around 2015 I covered a period where orders actually dropped to zero. That has galvanized management to "steady the ship" so that the dependency on that one area did not lead to such huge order rate swings.

As a result, Chart is now a very different company thanks to what management has accomplished. Further diversification will happen thanks to this merger that should steady earnings more while keeping the company involved with growth markets. The result is a growth company rather than a cyclical company that depended largely upon the natural gas cycle for large natural gas projects. This merger will continue that long-term journey.

Note that the aftermarket business is growing as a percentage of the business. This is likely the most countercyclical part of the business. The market likes smooth earnings. The growth of countercyclical business gives the company a steadiness that the market can easily understand.

The key to most Chart acquisitions and combinations is not the synergies savings (even though that is important). Instead, it is much more about the sales opportunities available to the combined company that were not available to the companies as separate entities. Usually, those opportunities far exceed any synergistic savings. In the past, those sales opportunities were a large component of the fast growth record of Chart. They will likely be a component of the growth record going forward.

What will likely follow the merger is a review of the sales opportunities for the combined company. Chart has generally combined the sales effort while keeping the manufacturing effort separate (and largely decentralized). Each of those product lines likely represents a past standalone company that is still free to pursue its own sales while at some level the combined effort results in more sales companywide.

As was the case in the past, post-merger company presentations will likely discuss "wins" of the combined sales effort in a communication strategy by management to assure the market of the benefits of the merger.

Similarly, the post-combination company will likely have a stock that responds to order intake (heavily) as is the case now.

Keeping each manufacturing location small makes quality control much easier to manage. It is actually a decentralized type of situation that is very similar to the Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B)(BRK.A) whereupon each "company" largely runs the show at the respective locations. This also makes rapid growth a less risky plan.

Shown above, is a couple of the potential examples of sales opportunities of the combined market. Keep in mind that Chart alone, has some fantastic growth opportunities to some relatively large markets that have enabled the growth record of the past.

What is shown above is actually some very traditional markets long-served likely by both companies separately. Chart has long strived to be a "one-stop shop". This merger moves the post-combined company closer to that reality for a larger number of customers.

Summary

I have followed Chart for decades ever since it was a $40 million dollar company. The company name is about to be retired and replaced by an entirely new name post combination. With one exception to its illustrious history, it has largely been a bonanza for investors throughout its existence.

Since Jill Evanko is going to move to the Chairman Of The Board, this is likely to lead to a smooth transition to relatively new management of the company that should benefit current shareholders. Jill Evanko is likely a wealth of information that should get the newly combined company off to a good start.

However, going forward, Scott Rowe will be the new CEO. He has some very big shoes to fill leading the combined company. Given that the transition is likely to be smooth the way things are, this is likely to remain a strong buy consideration for at least the near future.

The market may show some stock price hesitation until there is some assurance that the combined company will do as well as Chart has done on its own. At some point, Scott Rowe will be largely "on his own" to make his own track record. I hope that record is as good as what preceded him in the form of Chart management.

Risks

Jill Evanko, CEO and President, has a record of profitable growth at Chart that few at any management level can match. Replacing her is going to prove to be a challenge. We are going to find out (probably quickly) if Scott Rowe is up to the job.

The other consideration is that as a company grows like Chart has, the rate of growth inevitably slows. Already there is talk of a dividend along the lines of what Flowserve has done in the past. This likely means a hopefully smooth transition to an income and growth idea that frequently happens as companies get larger. But there is no assurance that the whole execution happens as I laid out.

The current political uncertainty is likely one motivating factor behind this transition. I think that the current situation is really negotiations that will largely replace the current uncertainty with something permanent and constructive. However, there is no assurance that will happen.

data.title: Alphabet: Current Valuation Is Lower Than March 2020
data.description:
Introduction

I have recommended buying the YTD dip in my previous Alphabet/Google (NASDAQ:GOOGL) (NASDAQ:GOOG) (TSX:GOOG:CA) article and my take remains the same despite the stock's underperformance since March 8. Google's leadership across a few large industries is undisputed, which is combined with being among the largest players in other thriving domains. It is difficult to believe that such a wide-moat FCF monster's P/E ratio is currently at just 20, expected to move closer to just 10 over the next few years. Therefore, the rating remains the same, 'Strong buy.'

Fundamental analysis

The biggest reason why the stock market is cautious about GOOGL is that there is intensifying competition from generative AI tools in the search engine market. I agree that such a risk exists, which I explain in more detail in the 'Mitigating factors' section of the article. However, this risk is extremely remote, and we should not forget that there are two robust indications that Google's dominance in search and digital advertising will remain intact in the foreseeable future.

Google Search continues delivering robust revenue growth every quarter. In addition, I want to remind you that Google's advertising revenue is generated not only via search engines but through YouTube as well. This asset's potential to drive digital advertising growth should not be underestimated because it boasts by far the largest market share among video streaming platforms.

The global video streaming market is poised to thrive over the next several years with a very aggressive CAGR. Optimistic growth forecasts look fair because younger generations prefer streaming platforms over linear television, according to a report from PwC. As traditional television is expected to experience a secular decline, it is reasonable to expect that the gap will be filled by video streaming offerings. With YouTube's market share way ahead of the second-place Netflix, Google is extremely well positioned to capitalize on this secular trend.

We also do not see a large discrepancy between Google's digital advertising revenue growth and the full-year 2025 estimated growth for the entire digital advertising industry. According to the first table presented in the 'Fundamental analysis', Google's core business's revenue grew by 9.8% year-over-year. This is more or less in line with Statista's forecast for the global digital advertising growth of 10.7% for this year. This indicates that Google's market share remains approximately the same in the global digital advertising industry.

Developments regarding the robotaxi market also look favorable for Google. The company's Waymo continues gaining traction as it plans to expand its geographical presence further. The robotaxi service is reportedly expected to launch in Atlanta and Orlando soon. Waymo is already present in a few large cities in the U.S., which makes its way ahead of Tesla in this market. To remind readers, Tesla is still yet to launch its robotaxi service, with a tentative start date of June 12. Having this strategic advantage over Tesla is a formidable asset for Google because the industry has exponential growth potential.

Google also performs extremely well in the cloud industry. Google Cloud is the third-largest player globally by market share, trailing AWS and Microsoft Azure. Moreover, Google demonstrates a strong commitment to close the gap with leaders because its cloud revenue demonstrated much more aggressive growth compared to competitors.

Aggressive innovation is the key to outperforming competitors in such a highly technologically sophisticated industry like the cloud. From this point of view, Google is positioned much better than its closest rivals because it has by far the largest net cash position among the three giants. Therefore, Google also has great potential to expand its market share in the cloud.

Valuation analysis

The forward P/E ratio trajectory looks extremely bullish. For a fundamentally strong, wide-moat company like Google, the current P/E of around 21 is already extremely low. In addition, the P/E ratio is expected to shrink dramatically over the next few years. The FY2028 estimated forward P/E ratio of 12.4 indicates an extremely cheap valuation.

Now, the DCF it is. I use the same 7.5% discount rate as in my previous analysis because there were no shifts in the Fed's monetary policy. I also maintain the same modest 4% constant growth rate for the terminal value (TV) calculation because of Google's rock-solid fundamentals.

Wall Street top-line forecasts for FY2026-2027 are represented by more than 30 reputable investment bankers, which makes it reliable enough to use as the DCF model's assumptions. After FY2027, I applied a steady revenue growth rate decline. Google's current FCF margin is 16.87%, which is the TTM performance. The AI race will certainly require Google to invest in CapEx aggressively over the next few years. Therefore, I am conservative with the FCF margin expansion trajectory and use the same FCF margin for the entire DCF horizon. Seeking Alpha suggests that the current outstanding share count is 12.13 billion.

With all these relatively conservative assumptions, Google's fair share price is around $208, meaning there is a 24% upside. This is a massive discount for a wide-moat technological leader like GOOGL. My confidence in this DCF model is also reinforced by the fact that my fair value estimate is only slightly higher than the average $199 target price from Wall Street.

The chart below says it all. Google's TTM P/E ratio is below 20, which already sounds unrealistic. I also want to emphasize that even during the COVID-19 panic in March 2020, Google's P/E ratio fell just slightly below 24. Thus, Google is currently valued much cheaper compared to the period of vast uncertainty triggered by an unprecedented pandemic which we had never seen before.

Mitigating factors

With the emergence of generative AI offerings that are capable of searching the Internet, competition risks significantly increased for Google. While the company has been dominating the search business over decades, its generative AI offering cannot boast the same dominance. Google's Gemini is a notable player in the generative AI field, but the competition is fierce there. Apart from the most widely known ChatGPT, there are various other emerging players backed by other Magnificent 7 companies like Grok, Llama, or Anthropic Claude. There are also thriving players that might be not that well-known but also accumulate their growth momentum. For example, Perplexity's valuation has grown by multiple times over the last few months and is currently worth $14 billion.

Apart from emerging generative AI players, some well-known large companies are also expanding their footprints in digital advertising. For example, Amazon's digital advertising unit is also demonstrating strong growth momentum, with a 17.7% year-over-year revenue growth in Q1 2025. Such a growth pace is very impressive, especially taking into account that this business' scale is already quite large with quarterly revenue far above $10 billion.

Conclusion

Despite potential competition risks from emerging generative AI players, I believe that Google's prospects for the next decade are intact. The company's business mix is stellar and the management's commitment to innovate and compete is rock-solid.

data.title: Golub Capital BDC's NAV, Valuation, And Dividend Versus 11 BDC Peers - Part 1 (Includes Recommendations As Of 5/30/2025)
data.description:
Focus of Article:

The focus of PART 1 of this article is to analyze Golub Capital BDC Inc.’s (NASDAQ:GBDC) recent results and compare a handful of the company’s metrics to 11 business development company (BDC) peers. This analysis will show past and current data with supporting documentation. Table 1 will compare GBDC’s recent net asset value (NAV) economic return (loss), adjusted net investment income (NII), stock price to annualized NII ratio, and percentage of total investment income attributable to capitalized payment-in-kind (PIK)/deferred interest income to the 11 BDC peers. Table 1 will also provide a premium (discount) to estimated CURRENT NAV analysis using stock prices as of 5/30/2025. Table 2 will compare GBDC’s investment portfolio (including several additional metrics) as of 12/31/2024 and 3/31/2025 to the 11 BDC peers.

I am writing this two-part article due to the continued requests that such an analysis be specifically performed on GBDC and some of the company’s BDC peers at periodic intervals. These BDC peers include Ares Capital Corp. (ARCC), Blackstone Secured Lending Fund (BXSL), Capital Southwest Corp. (CSWC), FS KKR Capital Corp. (FSK), Gladstone Investment Corp. (GAIN), Main Street Capital Corp. (MAIN), Oaktree Specialty Lending Corp. (OCSL), Blue Owl Capital Corp. (OBDC), SLR Investment Corp. (SLRC), TriplePoint Venture Growth BDC Corp. (TPVG), and Sixth Street Specialty Lending Corp. (TSLX). Directly due to low subscriber demand/popularity, I decided to drop coverage of MidCap Financial Investment Corp. (MFIC), PennantPark Floating Rate Capital Ltd. (PFLT), and Blackrock (BLK) TCP Capital Corp. (TCPC) in January 2025.

Understanding the characteristics of a company’s investment portfolio and operating performance can shed some light on which companies are overvalued or undervalued, strictly per a “numbers” analysis. This is not the only data that should be examined to initiate a position within a particular stock/sector. However, I believe this analysis would be a good “starting point” to begin a discussion on the topic. My Buy, Sell, or Hold recommendation and current price target for GBDC will be in the “Conclusions Drawn” section of the article. This includes providing a list of the BDC stocks I currently believe are undervalued (a buy recommendation), overvalued (a sell recommendation), or appropriately valued (a hold recommendation).

NAV, Economic Return (Loss), Current Premium (Discount) to NAV, and NII Analysis – Overview:

Let us start this analysis by getting accustomed to the information provided in Table 1 below. This will be beneficial when explaining how GBDC compares to the company’s 11 BDC peers regarding the metrics stated above. Due to the fact several BDC peers listed in Table 1 have a different fiscal year-end, all quarterly results are based on a calendar year-end. For instance, all metrics below are stated “Q1 2025” even though this does not correspond to every company’s fiscal year-end. Readers should be aware of this when the analysis is presented below.

Table 1a + 1b – NAV, Economic Return (Loss), Current Premium (Discount) to NAV, NII, and Capitalized PIK Analysis

(Source: Table created by me, obtaining historical stock prices from NASDAQ and each company’s NAV per share figures from the SEC’s EDGAR Database.)

Table 1 above provides the following information on GBDC and the 11 BDC peers (see each corresponding column): 1) NAV per share at the end of calendar Q4 2024; 2) NAV per share at the end of calendar Q1 2025; 3) NAV per share change during calendar Q1 2025 (percentage); 4) economic return (loss) (change in NAV and accrued dividend) during calendar Q1 2025 (percentage); 5) economic return (loss) during the trailing 12-months (percentage); 6) my estimated CURRENT NAV per share (NAV as of 5/30/2025); 7) stock price as of 5/30/2025; 8) 5/30/2025 premium (discount) to my estimated CURRENT NAV (percentage); 9) NII (or adjusted NII where applicable) per share during calendar Q1 2025; 10) NII (or adjusted NII where applicable) per share change versus the prior quarter; 11) NII (or adjusted NII where applicable) per share change versus calendar Q1 2024; 12) 5/30/2025 stock price to annualized NII ratio; and 13) percentage of total investment income attributable to capitalized PIK (deferred) interest income during calendar Q1 2025 (percentage).

Now that an overview has been provided, let us start the comparative analysis.

Analysis of GBDC:

Using Table 1 above as a reference, GBDC had an NAV of $15.13 per share at the end of calendar Q4 2024. GBDC had an NAV of $15.04 per share at the end of calendar Q1 2025. This calculates to a quarterly NAV decrease of ($0.09) per share or (0.59%). The majority of this NAV decrease was directly in relation to a minor net realized loss associated with 2 restructurings of previous non-accrual portfolio companies during the quarter. When including GBDC’s quarterly base dividend of $0.39 per share and no special periodic dividend, the company had an economic return (change in NAV and accrued dividends) of $0.30 per share or 1.98% for calendar Q1 2025. It should also be noted, that GBDC had a trailing 12- and 24-month economic return of 11.77% and 25.87%, respectively. Both percentages were slightly above the covered 12 BDC peer average (a positive catalyst/trend).

GBDC’s performance during the past four quarters was mainly attributable to the following three factors: 1) minor net overpayment of dividends (when including special periodic dividends) when compared to the company’s NII/adjusted NII; 2) modest net realized losses within a handful of exited/restructured portfolio companies; and 3) minor net unrealized appreciation within a majority of the company’s active investment portfolio. This is a good transition to the next topic of discussion, an analysis of GBDC’s investment portfolio (including several additional metrics) as of 12/31/2024 and 3/31/2025. To begin this analysis, Table 2 is provided below.

Tables 2a + 2b – Investment Portfolio Composition Analysis (Including Several Additional Metrics; 3/31/2025 Versus 12/31/2024)

(Source: Tables created by me, directly obtaining some figures/percentages from the SEC’s EDGAR Database [link provided below Table 1]. All remaining figures/percentages were calculated using data obtained within the SEC’s EDGAR Database.)

Using Tables 2a and 2b above as a reference, GBDC had 92% and less than 1% of the company’s investment portfolio in senior secured first- and second-lien loans as of 3/31/2025, respectively. As such, these types of loans comprised the majority of GBDC’s investment portfolio. When compared to the prior quarter, GBDC’s percentage of senior secured first- and second-lien loans remained unchanged. GBDC also had less than 1%, 0%, and 7% of the company’s investment portfolio in subordinated debt (unsecured loans), collateralized loan obligations (CLO)/credit-linked notes (CLN) (structured securitizations) + other, and equity/warrants, respectively. When compared to the prior quarter, GBDC’s percentage of subordinated debt (unsecured loans), CLO/CLN (structured securitizations) + other, and equity/warrants also remained unchanged. As such, there was not a notable shift in investment portfolio composition during calendar Q1 2025.

I would also point out, that GBDC’s proportion of second-lien loans and subordinated debt is very low when compared to the company’s 11 BDC peers. Simply put, this is generally a positive characteristic regarding potential credit risk/recoveries in a recessionary environment (consider general credit hierarchy). I believe GBDC’s below-average non-accrual percentages are direct evidence of the company’s more cautionary strategy regarding capital stack/credit hierarchy.

As of 3/31/2025, GBDC’s investment portfolio had a “fair market value (FMV) versus cost” ratio of 0.9941x. When compared to the 11 other BDC peers within this analysis, this ratio was slightly below the mean of 1.0106x (a minor negative factor/trend). When compared to a ratio of 0.9920x as of 12/31/2024, GBDC’s ratio very slightly increased during calendar Q1 2025. As noted above, this was mainly the result of partially realized losses in conjunction with 2 non-accrual restructurings, offset by minor unrealized appreciation within the remainder of GBDC’s investment portfolio. A more detailed “breakdown” of GBDC’s quarterly performance was recently provided to our Investing Group subscribers via a quarterly earnings assessment article.

GBDC had 1.2% and 0.7% of the company’s investment portfolio on “non-accrual” status as of 3/31/2025; based on its amortized cost basis and FMV, respectively. When compared to the 11 BDC peers as of 3/31/2025, GBDC’s amortized cost and FMV non-accrual percentage were slightly—modestly below the mean of 3.5% and 1.9%, respectively (a positive catalyst/trend).

Since the company’s initial public offering (IPO) in 2010, GBDC’s investment portfolio as of 3/31/2025 has generated a cumulative realized loss of ($0.44) per share (when based on a per-share count as of 3/31/2025).
