GBDC’s cumulative realized loss figure was slight—modestly more attractive when compared to the mean loss of ($0.82) per share (a positive catalyst/trend). I believe calculating a BDC’s cumulative realized gain (loss) per share amount provides an extremely useful metric when analyzing the long-term performance of management’s underwriting abilities, due diligence, expertise, and operational performance. This metric provides direct evidence that GBDC’s management team has, regarding a majority of instances, continued to find attractive debt/equity investments over a long period of time which, more times than not, have ultimately delivered attractive risk-adjusted returns.

As of 3/31/2025, 0.08% of GBDC’s portfolio had debt and equity investments within the oil and gas sector (based on FMV; including certain investments in the energy sector which had “oil and gas” characteristics and/or services closely linked to the sector). When compared to the 11 other BDC peers within this analysis, GBDC’s oil and gas exposure was slightly below the mean of 1.45%. When compared to the prior quarter, GBDC’s exposure to the oil and gas sector remained unchanged. Even though larger oil and gas companies benefited during most of 2022 from a net increase in commodity prices tied to inflation and the ongoing Russia/Ukraine conflict, I would point out that most sector prices have largely retraced during late 2022 – 2025. In addition, smaller/private oil and gas companies are not “out of the woods” (still high-cost outlays making breakeven prices higher). This could be a short- or long-term event, dependent upon many unknown geopolitical/macroeconomic variables. As such, I would remain a bit cautious considering the ramifications of this specific sector regarding high-yield/speculative-grade credit. I believe GBDC’s continued very low percentage attributed to oil and gas companies remains a positive catalyst/trend.

Once again, using Table 1 as a reference, GBDC reported an adjusted NII (which excludes merger-related premium amortization) of $0.388 per share during calendar Q1 2025. I prefer to track/utilize GBDC’s adjusted NII metric as opposed to NII (more indicative of net investment company taxable income (ICTI)). When comparing each company’s stock price as of 5/30/2025 to its annualized NII (or where applicable, adjusted NII), GBDC had the 7th lowest ratio at 9.80x. GBDC’s annualized adjusted NII ratio was slightly below the 12-peer ratio of 10.01x as of 5/30/2025 (a slightly positive catalyst/trend). Historically speaking, GBDC’s price-to-earnings ratio as of 5/30/2025 was attractive/low (a positive catalyst/trend).

During calendar Q1 2025, 6.88% of GBDC’s total investment income was attributable to capitalized PIK/deferred interest income. When compared to the 11 other BDC peers within this analysis, this was basically at the mean of 6.87%. I believe it is never a positive catalyst/trend when a BDC has any portion of its accrued income classified as being capitalized/deferred. Simply put, under GAAP, capitalized PIK interest/dividend income is revenue that is currently being “booked” but has not actually been received in cash yet (deferred). In a majority of cases, capitalized PIK interest income is paid in cash at the maturity of that particular loan/when a sale occurs. However, in my experience, more times than not, capitalized PIK interest income is a contractual amendment regarding a specific portfolio company that is, at the time, having operational difficulties (which increases the probability of the eventual inability to pay its loan obligations). This especially holds true when a specific debt investment had no capitalized PIK feature at the origination of a particular loan but is currently accruing 100% capitalized PIK interest income due to a loan modification/restructuring.

Simply put, in a majority of cases, it is a “slick” strategy of continuing to record accrued interest income only to write off this capitalized interest income at a later date; usually at loan maturity by classifying that “lost deferred interest” as a reduction in the debt investment’s proceeds (a realized loss) as opposed to lowering previously accrued income by the accumulated capitalized PIK balance. I am not stating this occurs all the time, but certain BDC peers tend to utilize this “phantom income” strategy regularly. In particular, Prospect Capital Corp. (PSEC) has been prone to this strategy to a greater degree over the years versus all the other BDC peers within this analysis, which was pointed out for years (and one of the reasons I dropped that BDC back in 2024). As such, it could be the case that capitalized PIK interest income is never “completely” received in cash upon maturity/when a sale occurs. In my professional opinion, if a BDC has a large/above-average portion of its investment income classified/accrued as capitalized PIK interest income, it should be seen as a potential concern regarding future performance/credit quality. In the end, one really just has to go “case-by-case” to determine the overall “viability” of a BDC actually eventually receiving this capitalized PIK/deferred interest income in the future. Something I continually monitor/track in my modeling.

As of 5/30/2025, GBDC’s stock price traded at $15.21 per share. When calculated, GBDC’s stock price was trading at a premium to my estimated CURRENT NAV (NAV as of 5/30/2025; $14.95 per share) of $0.26 per share, or 1.74%. This was modestly more attractive than the 12-BDC covered peer average of a premium of 10.89% (a positive catalyst/trend). I continue to believe GBDC should trade at a minor—modest premium to the company’s CURRENT NAV. As such, based on my proven valuation methodology over various interest rate/economic cycles, I currently believe GBDC is one of a handful of BDC peers that is deemed to be appropriately valued.

Comparison of GBDC’s NAV, Economic Return, Valuation, NII, and Other Metrics to 11 BDC Peers in Ranking Order:

The REIT Forum Feature

Conclusions Drawn (PART 1):

PART 1 of this article has analyzed GBDC and 11 other BDC peers in regard to the following metrics: 1) trailing 12-month economic return (loss) (a good indicator of recent overall performance); 2) a percentage of investments on non-accrual status as of 3/31/2025 (a good indicator of overall portfolio health/credit risk); 3) cumulative gain (loss) per share as of 3/31/2025 (a great indicator of long-term performance); 4) current premium (discount) to my estimated CURRENT NAV per share (NAV as of 5/30/2025) (a very good indicator of overall valuation); 5) current stock price to annualized NII ratio (a good indicator of overall valuation); and 6) a percentage of total investment income attributable to capitalized PIK (deferred) interest income (a good indicator of overall portfolio health/credit risk).

When compared to the 11 other BDC peers within this analysis, I believe GBDC continues to outperform a majority of the company’s BDC peers I currently cover. This includes but is not limited to, GBDC’s attractive adjusted NII per share (though, to remain unbiased, has net decreased over the prior several quarters [similar to most sector peers]), a modestly below average percentage of investments on non-accrual status as of 3/31/2025, a slightly—modestly more attractive cumulative realized gain (loss) per share amount as of 3/31/2025, a very low exposure to the oil and gas sector (including certain investments in the energy sector which had oil and gas characteristics and/or services closely linked to the sector), an above average trailing 12-month economic return percentage, and a very slightly more attractive stock price to annualized adjusted NII ratio (all positive catalysts/trends).

In addition, GBDC fairly recently (effective July 2023) lowered management fees from 1.375% of total assets less cash and cash equivalents to just 1.00% which is currently tied for the lowest percentage out of the externally managed covered BDC peers. Furthermore, effective January 2024, GBDC lowered the cap/max regarding the company’s income incentive fees from 20.0% of pre-incentive fee NII to 15.0% which is currently the lowest percentage out of the externally managed covered BDC peers. This includes continuing to have the highest income incentive fee “hurdle” rate (rate of return) of 8.00%. Simply put, this will continue to positively impact shareholders via lower proportionate fees versus all other externally managed BDC covered peers.

That said, to remain non-biased, this article also highlighted that GBDC had a near average FMV versus cost ratio and capitalized PIK interest income percentage (both neutral/slightly negative factors/trends). In my opinion, GBDC’s PIK interest income percentage continues to be the company’s biggest negative factor/trend (although this percentage has been relatively stable over the past 2 years [unlike a majority of covered BDC peers]).

Looking back, I previously correctly identified the high—very high probability of multiple GBDC base dividend increases and/or special periodic dividends during late 2022–2024. This was mainly due to the U.S. London Interbank Offered Rate (LIBOR)/Standard Overnight Financing Rate (SOFR)/PRIME very quickly moving past all floors on the asset side of the balance sheet, along with refinancing outstanding borrowings with longer-term, lower-cost debt on the liability side of the balance sheet, during 2021.

That said, to remain non-biased, during 2024–2025 one had/has to continue to monitor the weighted average annualized yield on new loan originations versus existing debt investments as there remains a pretty good “lag” in this specific metric. Something I continually track. In addition, I believe the majority of sector earnings peaked during late 2023 – early 2024 (recently plateaued). The rapid NII growth that sector peers experienced during 2022–2023 simply did not occur during 2024 (nor will occur during 2025). As the FOMC began to reduce the Federal Funds Rate (started in September 2024), sector earnings will GRADUALLY decrease over time. The severity of decreases will vary peer-to-peer (which I continuously project/model). This notion is already embedded in all price targets. Furthermore, credit risk will almost certainly continue to rise (albeit mildly) throughout the sector during 2025 – early 2026 but should begin to slowly ease later in 2026.

However, I continue to believe GBDC is better positioned to weather a potential minor—modest recession when compared to most sector peers (as highlighted in the analysis above). In addition, GBDC’s “cushion” to maintain the company’s previously increased base quarterly dividend per-share rate of $0.39 per share is fairly good. That said, as correctly pointed out 2 quarters ago, GBDC’s special periodic dividend would very likely net decrease over time (similar to most sector peers).

My BUY, SELL, or HOLD Recommendation:

From the analysis provided above, including additional factors not discussed within this article (additional metrics covered in PART 2), I currently rate GBDC as a SELL when I believe the company’s stock price is trading at or greater than a 7.5% premium to my projected CURRENT NAV (NAV as of 5/30/2025; $14.95 per share), a HOLD when trading at less than a 7.5% premium but less than a (2.5%) discount to my projected CURRENT NAV, and a BUY when trading at or greater than a (2.5%) discount to my projected CURRENT NAV.

Therefore, with a closing price as of 6/3/2025 of $15.02 per share, I currently rate GBDC as APPROPRIATELY VALUED from a stock price perspective.

As such, I currently believe GBDC is a HOLD recommendation.

My current price target for GBDC is approximately $16.05 per share. This is currently the price where my recommendation would change to a SELL. The current price where my HOLD recommendation would change to a BUY is approximately $14.60 per share. Put another way, the following are my CURRENT BUY, SELL, or HOLD per share recommendation ranges for GBDC (our Investing Group subscribers get this type of data on all 12 BDC (and 18 mortgage real estate investment trust [mREIT) stocks I currently cover on a weekly basis):

$16.05 per share or above = SELL (Overvalued)

$14.61 - $16.04 per share = HOLD (Appropriately Valued)

$13.11 - $14.60 per share = BUY (Undervalued)

$13.10 per share or below = STRONG BUY (Notably Undervalued).

BDC Sector Recommendations as of 5/30/2025:

Table 10 – Past and Current BDC Recommendations

(Source: Table created by me, including all past and present recommendations based on data obtained from the SEC’s EDGAR Database [link provided below Table 1a].)

Table 10 above provides the following information on GBDC and the 11 BDC peers (see each corresponding column): 1) 2/21/2020 BUY, SELL, or HOLD recommendation (pre-COVID-19 sell-off); 2) 3/31/2020 BUY, SELL, or HOLD recommendation (pre-COVID-19 rally); and 3) 5/30/2025 BUY, SELL, or HOLD recommendation range, relative to my estimated CURRENT NAV.

I currently have 0 BDCs rated as NOTABLY UNDERVALUED (STRONG BUY), 0 rated as UNDERVALUED (BUY), 8 rated as APPROPRIATELY VALUED (HOLD), 3 rated as OVERVALUED (SELL), and 1 as NOTABLY OVERVALUED (STRONG SELL). Simply put, certainly not as bullish as March–April 2020, during the summer of 2024, or more recently during April 2025. I currently believe the sector, as a whole, is appropriately valued (including GBDC) – slightly overvalued. I would just be mindful, knowing high-yield/speculative-grade credit spreads directly impact broader asset valuations. I continue to project a net widening of spreads during 2025. This is something I have continued to discuss with subscribers. Important to understand.

The analysis performed above does not provide “every” catalyst/factor to consider when choosing a BDC investment. However, I believe this analysis is a good starting point to begin a discussion on the topic. Additional metrics will be analyzed in PART 2 of this article. PART 2 will take a look at GBDC’s past and current dividend rates, yields, and other similar metrics and compare the results to the 14 other BDC peers. Several of these metrics have a direct impact on future operations/results as events unfold. This includes dividend projections for all 12 peers for calendar Q3 2025/each applicable company’s next set of dividend projections.

My Personal GBDC Past + Current Stock Disclosures:

The following are my GBDC past and current stock disclosures and total returns since I have been writing on Seeking Alpha (since 2013):

Table 11 – GBDC Past + Current Stock Disclosures/Returns

Source: Taken Directly from the REIT Forum’s © Spreadsheets/Data.

Final Note: All trades/investments I have performed over the past 8+ years have been disclosed to readers in “real-time” (that day at the latest) via Seeking Alpha and, more recently, the “live chat” feature of our Investing Group (which cannot be changed/altered). Beginning in January 2020, I transitioned all my real-time purchase and sale disclosures solely to subscribers of the REIT Forum. All applicable public articles will still have my “main ticker” purchase and sale disclosures (just not real-time alerts). At the end of May 2025, I had an unrealized/realized gain “success rate” of 90.0% and a total return (including dividends received) success rate of 96.3% out of 80 total past and present mREIT and BDC positions (updated monthly; multiple purchases/sales in one stock count as one overall position until fully closed out). I encourage other Seeking Alpha contributors to provide real-time buy and sell updates for their readers/subscribers, which would ultimately lead to greater transparency/credibility.

Simply put, a contributor’s/team’s recommendation track record should “count for something” and should always be considered when it comes to credibility/successful investing.

Understanding My/Our Valuation Methodology Regarding mREIT Common and BDC Stocks:

The basic “premise” around my/our recommendations in the mREIT common and BDC sectors is value. Regarding operational performance over the long term, there are above-average, average, and below-average mREIT and BDC stocks. That said, better-performing mREIT and BDC peers can be expensive to own, as well as being cheap. Just because a well-performing stock outperforms the company’s sector peers over the long term, this does not mean this stock should be owned at any price. As with any stock, there is a price range where the valuation is cheap, a price where the valuation is expensive, and a price where the valuation is appropriate. The same holds true with all mREIT common and BDC peers. As such, regarding my/our investing methodology, each mREIT common and BDC peer has their own unique BUY, SELL, or HOLD recommendation range (relative to estimated CURRENT BV/NAV). The better-performing mREITs and BDCs typically have a recommendation range at a premium to BV/NAV (varying percentages based on overall outperformance) and vice versa with the average/underperforming mREITs and BDCs (typically at a discount to estimated CURRENT BV/NAV).

Each company’s recommendation range is "pegged" to estimated CURRENT BV/NAV because this way subscribers/readers can track when each mREIT and BDC peer moves within the assigned recommendation ranges (daily if desired). That said, the underlying reason why I place each mREIT and BDC recommendation range at a different premium or (discount) to estimated CURRENT BV/NAV is based on roughly 15–20 catalysts which include both macroeconomic catalysts/factors and company-specific catalysts/factors (both positive and negative). This investing strategy is not for all market participants. For instance, not likely a “good fit” for extremely passive investors. For example, investors hold a position in a particular stock, no matter the price, for say a period of 5+ years. However, as shown throughout my articles written here at Seeking Alpha since 2013, in the vast majority of instances I have been able to enhance my personal total returns and/or minimize my personal total losses from specifically implementing this particular investing valuation methodology. I hope this provides some added clarity/understanding for new subscribers/readers regarding my valuation methodology utilized in the mREIT common and BDC sectors.

Each investor's BUY, SELL, or HOLD decision is based on one's risk tolerance, time horizon, and dividend income goals. My personal recommendation will not fit each reader’s current investing strategy. The factual information provided within this article is intended to help assist readers when it comes to investing strategies/decisions. Please disregard any minor “cosmetic” typos if/when applicable.

data.title: Meta's Strategic Nuclear Leap Bolsters The Bull Case
data.description:
Since my last Meta Platforms, Inc. (NASDAQ:META) analysis, the stock has gained 20% in price.

The new 20-year contract between Meta and Constellation Energy (CEG) for nuclear energy could not come at a better time. As energy demands increase and grid systems become fragile, Meta is leading the charge of Big Tech energy transformation to bolster American security. The U.S. electricity demand is expected to rise significantly as customers rapidly adopt data-intensive AI and robotics operations that require more power. This changing demand requires consideration of volatile pricing and grid instability due to intermittent renewable sources. Natural gas pricing is one example of this risk, as prices (NG1:COM) went from below $3/MMBtu (Million British Thermal Units) to over $9/MMBtu between 2021 and 2022 due to international crises and supply chain issues.

Meta's recent contract is a pivot towards energy security, providing fixed pricing of likely about $80/MWh (megawatt-hour) of carbon-free electricity, which is very favorable considering current market averages of $50/MWh or more. The average market price for renewable energy per megawatt-hour will almost certainly rise substantially as the autonomous revolution picks up steam. High demand for AI and robotics will likely lead to increasing energy prices amid a gold rush for energy providers who can acclimatize quickly. $80 might not be dirt-cheap now, but it certainly is on track to be in five to 10 years amid advanced tech proliferation at scale. Unlike many other renewable energy agreements, the Meta-Constellation nuclear agreement is reliable and supportive of strong financial management, as it crucially provides baseload power that doesn’t vary based on weather.

Meta's Power Purchase Agreement Fortifies Its Position In The AI Revolution

By entering into an agreement with Constellation, Meta ensured that it will receive 1,121 MW per year from the Clinton Nuclear Station, starting in 2027. The agreement would effectively hedge against the volatility of energy price fluctuations and provide predictable operating costs and budget stability for the company in long-term planning. Meta has lock-in pricing for energy of likely about $80/MWh (though it could be lower), which looks very good in comparison to their competitors, with Microsoft's Three Mile Island restart expected to cost around $110/MWh.

If the wholesale price of electricity goes to $100/MWh, based on the Meta-Constellation contract, Meta could save around $176 million in annual pricing (at 90% uptime—$196 million at 100% uptime), enhancing operating margins substantially. Should prices increase moderately to approximately $80/MWh, the contract achieves cost neutrality and delivers important budgeting predictability. In a downside case where market prices stabilize at $60/MWh in the long term, Meta would be responsible for a modest premium of $176 million on an annual basis (i.e., the price of long-term cost certainty). This coverage and limited downside exposure highlights the strategic rationale of agreeing to lock in stable and carbon-free electrical generation for long-term, secure AI access on Meta’s platforms.

Healthy Margins & Durable Growth

Meta's rapidly growing energy footprint (14,975 GWh [gigawatt-hours]) of energy in 2023 that could grow considerably through 2030 due to AI/data center build-outs) elevates the strategic rationale for fixed-cost procurement. Meta's expected annual energy costs will probably be over $1 billion and would very likely be more volatile without hedging.

By 2030, Meta's Clinton purchase power agreement will provide a degree of cost stability over approximately 10% of its expected total power needs, contributing substantially to margin predictability. Assuming volatility, fixed pricing allows Meta to accumulate savings over 10 years of $1 billion or greater in a base case, which should contribute 20-30 basis points annually to EBITDA margins. In isolation this may seem modest, but when viewed in aggregate it forms a powerful and important layer of earnings predictability and a reliability to free cash flow that underpins a stronger investment case. In other words, Meta can’t be the world’s top AI social media company without reliable energy to sustain it. Energy is the soul of the autonomous revolution.

Widespread Positive Multipolar Sentiment Drives Valuation Upside

Meta's proactive approach to energy risk management warrants expanding valuation multiples as market sentiment compounds positively. Meta's trailing 12-month normalized price-to-earnings ratio is around 25x, indicating the market’s enthusiasm for the company’s long-term earnings growth. The addition of reduced volatility and enhanced ESG (environmental, social, and governance) positioning is one foundational element, among many, that justifies the price-to-earnings ratio expanding toward 30x. A price target of around $800 within 12 months is easily achievable, based on normalized trailing 12-month earnings per share of $27 in the middle of 2026.

Tesla may look a little expensive compared to its moving averages, but this doesn't really concern me due to the long-term growth thesis, shrewd management team, and AI optionality that is almost unquantifiable at this stage. I'm stoutly bullish, and I think we should expect Meta to continue to trade along and above its 50-week moving average indefinitely during the AI and robotics era. Moments of growth stagnation will likely be supported by shareholder-friendly actions like buybacks and dividend increases, keeping sentiment elevated.

Meta’s Nuclear Initiative Shows Bold & Visionary Commitment

Compared to its peer tech companies—Microsoft (MSFT), Google (GOOGL, GOOG), and Amazon (AMZN)—Meta’s deal introduces unique and immediate operational benefits that lead the way in terms of reliability, cost of a new energy source, and climate leadership. Google's and Amazon’s nuclear strategies consist of advanced reactors and small modular reactors, with projects that will not even be operational until the 2030s. Microsoft’s partnership to restart Three Mile Island comes with substantial upfront costs and technical uncertainty. Meta has a uniquely advantageous market position in terms of well-timed, relatively low-cost, and secure nuclear energy.

With operational nuclear energy beginning in 2027, Meta will realize a first-mover advantage in its marketplace, delivering a continuous and reliable power supply crucial for scaling AI computing workloads. Meta has a competitive moat, and energy strategies like this are the foundation of the fortress. By the time 2027 arrives, Meta can couple firm, clean, baseload capacity before their competitors can, which makes its shareholders less vulnerable to price and reliability risk.

Navigating Uncertainties Is Part Of The Journey

Meta's nuclear road map is not without risks, but these are largely surmountable and supported by strong macroeconomic tailwinds regarding consensus support for energy security amid massive AI demand and geopolitical uncertainties.

Regulatory risk is also prominent. The long-term viability of Meta's nuclear positioning—and its cost-effectiveness—will depend upon the continued pro-nuclear macro context and the timely relicensing of the Clinton plant when its current agreement expires. Even though the political environment is friendly toward nuclear today, political opposition to the energy form from high-intensity ESG proponents could cause road blocks. This would most likely be due to people focusing on the nuclear energy risk and waste level compared to generally safer forms of generation like solar. This could pose demand constraints for nuclear power and lower its cost, leading to Meta's fixed-price nuclear strategy underperforming.

There is a geopolitical risk as well; while it is unlikely, a disruption in the global uranium supply chain could present operational challenges. Meta can minimize that possibility by helping to add more domestic enrichment capacity and working with other fuel sources. Operationally, unplanned outages at Clinton could cause Meta to go out to the market and purchase replacement power (probably at a premium). That said, an agreement that allows for an alternative clean energy credit or compensation would mitigate some of the potential burden. In looking to diversify via additional nuclear and renewable projects with strong contracts, Meta is well positioned to deal with any operational uncertainty.

I want to keep it clear in the front of your mind that Meta's nuclear contract enhances its investment attractiveness by giving it a rare type of resiliency against both macroeconomic and operational risk. Predictable energy costs reduce earnings volatility and improve the steady reliability and cash flow that Meta can return to its investors during volatile times. This increased stability provides justification for a lower discount rate (it creates less risk inherently), which contributes to overall increased intrinsic value. Second, the contract gives Meta a first-mover advantage within a bleeding-edge trend in zero-carbon, sustainable, long-term, and reliable energy sourcing from nuclear. Although nuclear is contentious in some ESG circles, this will sway many ESG-mandated funds and investors to be bullish on where Meta is headed.

Conclusion: Strong Buy

Meta Platforms' nuclear deal with Constellation is not a traditional energy procurement agreement, but rather a strategy that leaned into foresight and elite corporate stewardship with agile and margin-sensitive execution. The deal synched energy resiliency with operational predictability and ESG leadership into a cohesive strategy around the company's core business of long-term AI-empowered social media dominance. It’s a deal that was born out of visualized looming weakness that was channeled into present strength. This is yet another reason that makes bullish valuations of Meta both valid and worthy. This is truly the inkling of an emerging narrative—a technology behemoth on a defined path to proactively reshape its risk profile through private-access clean energy infrastructure. If the strategy evolves more abundantly over time, Meta will not just be resilient to macroeconomic volatility, but it will thrive as the dominant global AI social media empire for decades to come.
